blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: There are trials. I follow them. I've been watching them since I was diagnosed — there are a few that are promising, gene therapy pathways, some structural repair approaches. The science is moving.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Right now, nothing that would reverse it. Nothing that would change the architecture. The medication manages it — it slows the progression significantly. I'm doing well, by the standards they have for this. But there's no cure. Not yet.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That's how I say it. Not yet. It helps.
He nods. Just once. He is cataloging things — she can see it — filing the information carefully, the way he files things he intends to carry with him.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: How long? If the medication keeps working — what do the doctors say?
She was expecting this question. She's been sitting with the answer for three years.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: They talk in ranges. If things stay on this track — if the medication keeps doing what it's doing — they say ten to twenty years is realistic. Possibly longer. They're careful not to promise.
The gondola reaches the top of the wheel. The world is very wide from here.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I might have decades. I might not. I live in between. I've learned to live there.
